studyMetadata.title: Conversion
studyMetadata.subtitle: New not (just) Nice
studyMetadata.description:
A nine-session Bible study exploring what genuine conversion means – and why it matters.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.
studyMetadata.author: Rick Creighton
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Conversion: New not (just) Nice”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: Nice — warm parchment and ink palette. Inspired by aged paper, calligraphy, and the tactile feel of a well-used study Bible. Earthy sepia tones with gold accent suggest careful scholarship and contemplative reading. Serious but inviting.

elements.text:
Suppose you were introduced to two men. Andrew is a man of strong moral standards – serious in his study of the Bible, a generous host, widely respected in his community. Ben is wealthy, but he has made his money from a combination of fraud, and barely legal exploitation of others. His neighbours avoid him.

But now suppose you find out that Andrew’s real name is Simon the Pharisee (Luke 7:36–50) and Ben’s real name is Zacchaeus (Luke 19:1–10). Simon was ‘nice’. Zacchaeus was ‘new’. Niceness is not the same thing.

elements.question: • Doesn’t ‘conversion’ have negative connotations today?
elements.answer:
In some countries, the word conversion has become associated in popular culture with so-called ‘conversion therapy’ – discredited attempts to change a person’s sexual orientation.


But the word itself is far older and richer than that misuse. In its biblical sense, conversion describes the most profound transformation a human being can undergo: a turning from darkness to light, from the power of Satan to God (Acts 26:18). It isn’t something done to a person against their will, and which that person regrets — it is something God does in a person, and which that person embraces.


This study uses the word in its full biblical sense. Genuine conversion isn’t coercion — it’s liberation.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Now a certain man, a Pharisee named Nicodemus, who was a member of the Jewish ruling council, <sup>2</sup>came to Jesus at night and said to him, “Rabbi, we know that you are a teacher who has come from God. For no one could perform the miraculous signs that you do unless God is with him.” <sup>3</sup>Jesus replied, “I tell you the solemn truth, unless a person is born from above, he cannot see the kingdom of God.” <sup>4</sup>Nicodemus said to him, “How can a man be born when he is old? He cannot enter his mother’s womb and be born a second time, can he?” <sup>5</sup>Jesus answered, “I tell you the solemn truth, unless a person is born of water and spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God. <sup>6</sup>What is born of the flesh is flesh, and what is born of the Spirit is spirit. <sup>7</sup>Do not be amazed that I said to you, ‘You must all be born from above.’

elements.question: What does Jesus say is required to see the kingdom of God?
Why is being ‘born again’ (or ‘born from above’) more radical than being more moral?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Though I myself could have confidence in the flesh. If someone thinks he has good reasons to put confidence in human distinctions, I have more: <sup>5</sup>I was circumcised on the eighth day, from the people of Israel and the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews. I lived according to the law as a Pharisee. <sup>6</sup>In my zeal for God I persecuted the church. According to the righteousness stipulated in the law I was blameless.

elements.question: What religious qualifications did Paul have?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>But these assets I have come to regard as liabilities because of Christ. <sup>8</sup>More than that, I now regard all things as liabilities compared to the far greater value of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have suffered the loss of all things — indeed, I regard them as dung! — that I may gain Christ,

elements.question: How does he now regard them?

elements.passageText: I regard them as dung! — that I may gain Christ, 9and be found in him, not because I have my own righteousness derived from the law, but because I have the righteousness that comes by way of Christ’s faithfulness — a righteousness from God that is in fact based on Christ’s faithfulness.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Most people assume conversion means adopting religious habits, cleaning up your behaviour, or having an emotional experience.


The Bible means something more radical: not self-improvement, but new life from God. Not a decision to be better – a resurrection.

elements.question: What does he say is the only true basis of righteousness?

elements.text: Niceness can smarten up the outside, but only new birth changes the heart. ‘Conversion’ refers to this new birth. It is God making dead people alive. It includes turning from sin, trusting in Christ, and receiving new life from God.

elements.passageText: You know that I did not hold back from proclaiming to you anything that would be helpful, and from teaching you publicly and from house to house, 21testifying to both Jews and Greeks about repentance toward God and faith in our Lord Jesus.

elements.question: What two responses does Paul summarise as his message?
How do repentance and faith relate to each other?

elements.passageText: 9For people everywhere report how you welcomed us and how you turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God and to wait for his Son from heaven, whom he raised from the dead, Jesus our deliverer from the coming wrath.

elements.question: What did the Thessalonians turn from – and to?
What does this show us about the nature of conversion?

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>So then, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; what is old has passed away — look, what is new has come!

elements.question: What happens to someone who is in Christ?
How does this go beyond just change of behaviour?

elements.text: Conversion is not just a religious milestone, or an emotional experience, or a moral improvement plan. It is God making sinners new. And that only makes sense if something is deeply wrong with us – if the problem is serious enough to need it. It is. Our problem is not just bad habits – not just spiritual sickness, but spiritual death.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>And although you were dead in your transgressions and sins, <sup>2</sup>in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the kingdom of the air, the ruler of the spirit that is now energizing the sons of disobedience, <sup>3</sup>among whom all of us also formerly lived out our lives in the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath even as the rest…

elements.question: How does Paul describe our condition apart from Christ?

elements.passageText: 4But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, even though we were dead in transgressions, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you are saved!

elements.question: Who takes the initiative?

elements.passageText: But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, 5even though we were dead in transgressions, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you are saved! —

elements.question: What does God do?
Why is this described as ‘grace’?

elements.passageText: For the outlook of the flesh is death, but the outlook of the Spirit is life and peace,) 7because the outlook of the flesh is hostile to God, for it does not submit to the law of God, nor is it able to do so. 8Those who are in the flesh cannot please God.

elements.question: What is the mind set on the flesh like? What can it not do?
Why does this mean we need more than self-improvement?

elements.text:
We do not need polishing. We need resurrection. Simon had everything that looked like godliness – and it counted for nothing. Zacchaeus had none of it – and was made new. Jesus did not come to make nice people slightly better. He came ‘to seek and to save the lost.’

The question this leaves you with isn’t ‘Am I a decent person?’ but ‘Have I been made new?’ If you’re not sure, that’s worth spending time thinking about. The same Jesus who sought out Zacchaeus is not hard to find.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Think of someone who seemed very ‘together’ spiritually – but whose life didn’t show evidence of conversion. What gave you that impression? What does that say about your assumptions?
Before doing this study, what did you think conversion meant? Has anything shifted in how you understand it? What feels most challenging or most encouraging about what you’ve discovered?
How do you know that you are truly converted? What would you point to – and what makes you uncertain?

elements.text:
James had tried everything. He’d quit drinking twice – once for eight months. He’d ended the relationship that kept pulling him back into old patterns. He’d started going to the gym. He’d even started going to church, which surprised him as much as anyone. Each time, something changed for a while. Then, slowly, it didn’t.

He wasn’t a bad man. Everyone said so. He just couldn’t seem to become the person he wanted to be. And the older he got, the more he suspected the problem wasn’t his circumstances, or willpower, or bad luck. The problem was him.

James’ problems were real. But the Bible’s diagnosis goes deeper than any of them: our core problem isn’t that we’re struggling, or confused, or weakened by bad influences. It’s that we are, spiritually speaking, dead. Not just sick, but dead. Until we grasp this, conversion seems excessive. Once we grasp it, it’s the only thing that makes sense.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Have mercy on me, O God, because of your loyal love; because of your great compassion, wipe away my rebellious acts. <sup>2</sup>Wash away my wrongdoing. Cleanse me of my sin. <sup>3</sup>For I am aware of my rebellious acts; I am forever conscious of my sin. <sup>4</sup>Against you — you above all — I have sinned; I have done what is evil in your sight. So you are just when you confront me; you are right when you condemn me.

elements.question: Who does David say he has ultimately sinned against?
What does this teach us about the true nature of sin?

elements.text: Sin is not just wrongdoing. It is personal – it is rebellion against our Creator. It is not first about breaking rules. It is about rejecting God. That is a different kind of problem.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>The human mind is more deceitful than anything else. It is incurably bad. Who can understand it?

elements.question: How is the human heart described?
What warning does this give about trusting ourselves, or following our hearts?

elements.text: Sin runs deep, all the way through us. That’s not to say we are all as bad as we could possibly be – not at all. But no part of us escapes sin’s reach. It shapes what we want, what we think, how we see ourselves and others. The problem isn’t just our actions. It’s us.

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>And even though you were dead in your transgressions and in the uncircumcision of your flesh, he nevertheless made you alive with him, having forgiven all your transgressions.

elements.question: What was our condition apart from Christ?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>And although you were dead in your transgressions and sins, <sup>2</sup>in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the kingdom of the air, the ruler of the spirit that is now energizing the sons of disobedience, <sup>3</sup>among whom all of us also formerly lived out our lives in the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath even as the rest…

elements.question: What three influences shape our condition apart from Christ?

elements.text: Perhaps the most confronting biblical description is this: we are dead. Dead people do not revive themselves. Conversion cannot be self-improvement. It must be resurrection.

elements.passageText: For the outlook of the flesh is death, but the outlook of the Spirit is life and peace, 7because the outlook of the flesh is hostile to God, for it does not submit to the law of God, nor is it able to do so.

elements.question: What is the mind set on the flesh like? Is it just weak, or something more?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>Those who are in the flesh cannot please God.

elements.question: What is it unable to do?

• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Wrath sounds like a loss of temper – God as an angry deity needing to be placated. But divine wrath is the settled, holy opposition of a good God to everything that destroys what he loves.


A God who was indifferent to evil would not be good. Wrath isn’t the opposite of love. In many ways, it’s the proof of it.

elements.passageText: For the outlook of the flesh is death, but the outlook of the Spirit is life and peace,) 7because the outlook of the flesh is hostile to God, for it does not submit to the law of God, nor is it able to do so. 8Those who are in the flesh cannot please God.

elements.question: Why does this rule out the possibility of us saving ourselves by our own moral effort?

elements.text: Our problem is not just that we do not try hard enough. It is that, left to ourselves, we don’t truly want God. We can be generous, kind, even religious – and still be living entirely for ourselves. Self is the problem. And self cannot fix self.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness,

elements.question: What is revealed from heaven? And against whom?
Why is wrath a necessary part of God’s character?

elements.passageText: And although you were dead in your offenses and sins, in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the domain of the air, the ruler of the spirit that is now energizing the sons of disobedience, 3among whom all of us also formerly lived out our lives in the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath even as the rest…

elements.question: What does it mean to be ‘children of wrath’?
Why is this a relational as well as a legal problem?

elements.text:
The seriousness of sin is seen in God’s holy response to it. If God didn’t care about evil, he would not be good. His wrath is not uncontrolled anger – it is his settled, holy opposition to sin.

And yet. The same Bible that diagnoses us so honestly does not leave us there. The diagnosis is not meant to crush you – it’s meant to show you what kind of help you actually need. You don’t need to try harder, or think better of yourself, or make a few improvements. You need rescue. You need someone to do for you what you cannot do for yourself.

God has looked at our condition fully – and moved toward us anyway. That is where the next chapter begins.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Which aspect of the Bible’s diagnosis do you find hardest to accept about yourself – guilt, corruption, inability, or wrath? What makes that one feel most uncomfortable or most unreal?
Is there a type of person – or a category of sin – toward whom you find it easy to feel superior? What does the Bible’s picture of your own sin say to that?

In what ways are you tempted to minimise the seriousness of sin in your own life? Can you think of a specific sin you have been calling ‘small’ – and what would it mean to take it as seriously as the Bible does?

chapters.chapterTitle: God, who is Rich in Mercy

elements.text:
When Joy was seventeen, she ran away from home. Not dramatically – no argument, no slammed door. She just stopped coming back. New city, new name almost, new life. She told herself she didn’t need her family. That chapter was closed.

Years later, she found a letter. Her mother had been writing to her, every month, to the last known address. Every month for eleven years. Joy had never received a single one. She sat in her car in a car park and read through them in order. Her mother had never stopped looking. Had never assumed silence meant Joy didn’t matter. Had never, not once, written her off.

Joy had walked away. Her mother had never stopped reaching.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, <sup>5</sup>even though we were dead in transgressions, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you are saved!

elements.question: Why does God take the initiative to make peace with us?

elements.passageText: 9He is the one who saved us and called us with a holy calling, not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace, granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began, but now made visible through the appearing of our Savior Christ Jesus, who has abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel.

elements.question: Why (and when) did God choose to save us?

elements.text: God did not save us because he saw something promising in us. He saved us according to his own purpose – a purpose decided not just before we had done anything, but before we even existed. We don’t work our way to God. God moves first. His mercy is not a reward. It is a rescue.

elements.passageText: <sup>37</sup>Everyone whom the Father gives me will come to me, and the one who comes to me I will never send away. … <sup>44</sup>No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him, and I will raise him up at the last day.

elements.question: Who comes to Jesus?

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>For he says to Moses: “I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.” <sup>16</sup>So then, it does not depend on human desire or exertion, but on God who shows mercy.

elements.question: On whom does God have mercy? What does it depend on?

elements.text: Left to ourselves, we would not come to God. We wouldn’t even want to. But God does not leave us to ourselves. He comes for us.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>But to all who have received him — those who believe in his name — he has given the right to become God’s children — <sup>13</sup>children not born by human parents or by human desire or a husband’s decision, but by God.

elements.question: Who are given the right to become children of God?
What three things does the new birth not depend on? What does it depend on?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>By his sovereign plan he gave us birth through the message of truth, that we would be a kind of firstfruits of all he created.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
‘Grace’ sounds like God being lenient – turning a blind eye, or grading on a curve. But grace isn’t God lowering his standards. It’s God acting in our favour when we had no right to expect it and nothing to offer in return.


It’s not a reward for the almost-good-enough. It’s a rescue for the lost. It is God’s free gift to us.

elements.question: Who brought us to new birth? And how?

elements.text: God doesn’t top up a spiritual life that was already flickering. He creates life where there was none. And he does it through the gospel — the message of Jesus Christ, spoken and heard.

elements.passageText: 1Now I want to make clear for you, brothers and sisters, the gospel I preached to you, that you received and on which you stand, 2and by which you are being saved, if you hold firmly to the message I preached to you — unless you believed in vain. 3For I passed on to you as of first importance what I also received — that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures, 4and that he was buried, and that he was raised on the third day according to the scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve.

elements.question: What does Paul summarise as ‘the gospel’?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>For I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is God’s power for salvation to everyone who believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.

elements.question: What is God’s power for salvation?

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>For the righteousness of God is revealed in the gospel from faith to faith, just as it is written, “The righteous by faith will live.”

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The idea that Christ ‘died for us’ is familiar enough to lose its force. It’s sometimes softened into Christ dying as a gesture of solidarity, or a demonstration of love.


But the Bible’s language is more specific: he died in our place, bearing the penalty that was ours. Someone else stood where we should have stood. That’s not just moving – it’s the hinge on which everything else turns.

elements.question: What is revealed in the gospel? And why do we need it?

elements.text: The gospel is not advice about how to live. It is news about what God has done in Christ. We are called to speak it clearly. But the power to change a life is not in our delivery. It’s in the message itself.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>God made the one who did not know sin to be sin for us, so that in him we would become the righteousness of God.

elements.question: What did God make Christ to be?
What does this teach us about substitution and salvation?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>Because Christ also suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, to bring you to God, by being put to death in the flesh but by being made alive in the spirit.

elements.question: Why did Christ suffer? And who for?

elements.text: Conversion happens because Christ has done all that’s needed. We don’t convert ourselves into God’s favour, we don’t earn it. We trust in the One who has secured it. The work is done. We receive it.

elements.passageText: <sup>30</sup>Therefore, although God has overlooked such times of ignorance, he now commands all people everywhere to repent, <sup>31</sup>because he has set a day on which he is going to judge the world in righteousness, by a man whom he designated, having provided proof to everyone by raising him from the dead.

elements.question: What does God now command? And why is it urgent?

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>How are they to call on one they have not believed in? And how are they to believe in one they have not heard of? And how are they to hear without someone preaching to them? <sup>15</sup>And how are they to preach unless they are sent? As it is written, “How timely is the arrival of those who proclaim the good news.” <sup>16</sup>But not all have obeyed the good news, for Isaiah says, “Lord, who has believed our report?” <sup>17</sup>Consequently faith comes from what is heard, and what is heard comes through the preached word of Christ.

elements.question: How does someone come to believe?

elements.text:
None of this means we are simply passive. God’s grace doesn’t bypass us – it reaches us. And when it reaches us, it calls for a real response: repentance and faith. Conversion is not the result of a slick presentation, or a moment of emotional pressure, or our own spiritual willpower. It is something more wonderful than any of those things.

Think of Joy, sitting in that car park, reading letters she had never known existed. The reaching had been going on for years before she knew it. But the moment she read them, a response was called for. She could not simply go on as before.

It is like that with us. It is God himself – rich in mercy, moved by love – who has been reaching toward us. He has already spoken. The question is whether you have come to believe it yet – and whether, today, you are living like someone who has come home.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Has there been a moment when you realised your coming to faith wasn’t down to your own searching or wisdom – that God had been at work before you knew it? What did that feel like?
Think of someone in your life whose heart seems closed to God. God takes the initiative and he can reach anyone – how does that truth change how you pray for them or speak to them?
When you share your faith (or think about doing so), is Christ’s death and what it achieved central – or do you tend to lead with something else (your own story, etc.)? What does that tell you?

elements.text:
For three years, Tom had known he needed to sort things out with his dad. The falling-out had been his fault; he knew that. He’d rehearsed the conversation a hundred times. Every Christmas, every birthday, he’d feel the weight of it. He genuinely wanted things to be different. But he never made the call.

Feeling sorry wasn’t the problem. He was sorry. He just hadn’t turned around. One night he finally did. He drove to his father’s house, knocked on the door, and said the things he’d been saying only to himself for three years. It was the same words he’d rehearsed. But this time, he was actually there.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Repentance is often confused with feeling guilty, or promising to try harder. But you can feel terrible about something and never actually change.


Real repentance is a change of mind and a change of direction. Not just a feeling, but a turning. Regret looks inward. Repentance turns toward God.

elements.passageText: Then he opened their minds so they could understand the scriptures, 46and said to them, “Thus it stands written that the Christ would suffer and would rise from the dead on the third day, 47and repentance for the forgiveness of sins would be proclaimed in his name to all nations, beginning from Jerusalem.

elements.question: What is to be proclaimed in Jesus’ name?

elements.passageText: <sup>30</sup>“Therefore I will judge each person according to his conduct, O house of Israel, declares the Sovereign LORD. Repent and turn from all your rebellious deeds; they will not be a stumbling block bringing iniquity upon you. <sup>31</sup>Throw away all your sins you have committed and fashion yourselves a new heart and a new spirit! Why should you die, O house of Israel? <sup>32</sup>For I take no pleasure in the death of anyone, declares the Sovereign LORD. Repent and live!

elements.question: Why is repentance urgent?

elements.text: Repentance is more than regret. We’ve all felt bad without changing. Real repentance is a turning – a change of direction, not just of feeling. It is a decisive turning from sin toward God. It’s not optional, and it’s not reserved for people who’ve done especially terrible things. It is God’s call to every one of us. It starts on the inside – a genuine change of heart and mind – but it never stays there.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Now I rejoice — not because you were grieved, but because you were grieved to repentance. For you were grieved in a godly way, so that you were not harmed by us in any way. <sup>10</sup>For sadness as intended by God produces a repentance that leads to salvation, leaving no regret, but worldly sadness brings about death.

elements.question: What two kinds of grief does Paul distinguish?
How can we tell the difference?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>And see to it that no one becomes an immoral or godless person like Esau, who sold his own birthright for a single meal. <sup>17</sup>For you know that later when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no opportunity for repentance, although he sought the blessing with tears.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Faith is often treated as a feeling (‘I just need to believe more strongly’) or vague spiritual optimism. But biblical faith isn’t about the intensity of your trust – it’s about the object of it.


Weak faith in a reliable person is better than strong confidence in the wrong one. Faith means entrusting yourself to a specific person – Jesus – and what he has specifically done. Faith means depending on him.

elements.question: What did Esau seek? What could he not find?

elements.text:
You can feel sorrow without turning to God. Tears are not the same as repentance. Repentance isn’t self-hatred. It’s not a promise to try harder. It is turning from sin because we see it for what it is – rebellion against a holy and gracious God.

Repentance and faith are two sides of the same coin. Repentance is letting go – loosening your grip on sin and self-sufficiency. Faith is reaching out – trusting your weight to Christ. They belong together; you can’t genuinely do one without the other. Faith isn’t a feeling, or a vague hope. It is trust in a specific person – Jesus – and in what he has specifically done. On days when that trust feels weak, what matters is not the strength of our grip. It is what we are holding on to, and who is holding on to us.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life. <sup>17</sup>For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world should be saved through him. <sup>18</sup>The one who believes in him is not condemned. The one who does not believe has been condemned already, because he has not believed in the name of the one and only Son of God.

elements.question: What is promised to whoever believes? And what if we don’t?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>But these assets I have come to regard as liabilities because of Christ. <sup>8</sup>More than that, I now regard all things as liabilities compared to the far greater value of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have suffered the loss of all things — indeed, I regard them as dung!

elements.question: What does Paul give up?

elements.passageText: More than that, I now regard all things as liabilities compared to the far greater value of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have suffered the loss of all things — indeed, I regard them as dung! — that I may gain Christ, 9and be found in him, not because I have my own righteousness derived from the law, but because I have the righteousness that comes by way of Christ’s faithfulness — a righteousness from God that is in fact based on Christ’s faithfulness.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear these words?
  • What do you think they really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
‘Justified’ and ‘righteous’ are sometimes read as meaning ‘good enough’ or ‘morally improved’ – or even as meaning ‘self-righteous.’ But in Scripture, to be justified means to be declared right with God – declared innocent – not because you’ve become righteous, but because Christ’s righteousness has been credited to you.


It’s a legal verdict, not a character assessment. God doesn’t declare you righteous because you are. He makes you righteous by declaring it.

elements.question: What does he desire instead?

elements.text: Faith is not just agreeing that facts are true. It is entrusting ourselves to Christ alone.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God; <sup>9</sup>it is not from works, so that no one can boast.

elements.question: By what are we saved? And through what?
What role do works play? And what does this do to boasting?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Now to the one who works, his pay is not credited due to grace but due to obligation. <sup>5</sup>But to the one who does not work, but believes in the one who declares the ungodly righteous, his faith is credited as righteousness.

elements.question: What is the difference between wages and a gift?
How are the ungodly justified?

elements.text: Faith does not earn salvation. It receives it.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Now after John was imprisoned, Jesus went into Galilee and proclaimed the gospel of God. <sup>15</sup>He said, “The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe the gospel!”

elements.question: What two responses does Jesus command?
Why do these belong together?

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>Then he said to them all, “If anyone wants to become my follower, he must deny himself, take up his cross daily, and follow me. <sup>24</sup>For whoever wants to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will save it. <sup>25</sup>For what does it benefit a person if he gains the whole world but loses or forfeits himself?

elements.question: What does Jesus say about following him? What’s at stake?

elements.text:
Repentance and faith are inseparable. You can’t cling to sin and cling to Christ at the same time. Repentance without faith can spiral into despair – ‘I’ve turned from sin, but there’s nothing to turn to.’ And faith without repentance can become a kind of comfortable excuse – ‘God will forgive me, so it doesn’t really matter how I live.’ True conversion holds both together.

Conversion is not adding Jesus to our existing priorities. It is reordering our lives around him. Repentance is a decisive turning from sin, and faith is personal trust in Christ and his finished work. We don’t earn our salvation by producing them – they are gifts of God’s grace.

Maybe you can look back to a moment when this turn happened for you – a specific day, or a slow dawning you couldn’t pin to a date. Or maybe you are honestly not sure whether it has happened at all. Either way, the invitation standing in front of you right now is the same one it has always been: turn from sin and trust in Christ. Not eventually. Today. That is what conversion is – and it is available to you right now.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Can you think of a time when you felt genuine sorrow about a sin but didn’t actually turn from it? What was going on? What is the difference between what you felt then and what repentance would have looked like?
Do you ever try to ‘make up for’ sin through religious activity rather than returning to Christ in repentance and faith?
If someone who knows you well were asked whether your life shows repentance and faith, what might they say?

elements.text:
Nobody expected Daniel to change. Least of all Daniel. He’d been in prison three times by his thirties. People who loved him had learned, the hard way, to keep expectations low. His sister had stopped hoping out loud. His mother still prayed, but quietly, mostly to herself.

Then something happened that no one could fully explain – including Daniel. He didn’t clean up his life and then become a Christian. Something shifted in him that he hadn’t chosen and couldn’t have manufactured. Old things started to feel hollow. He found himself wanting what he’d always mocked. His sister noticed first. She said it was like talking to a different person. Same face. Different person.

What had happened? And why then, and not the twenty times before?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this phrase?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Culturally, ‘born again’ has come to mean a particular type of religious personality – emotional, conservative, or fervent. But Jesus uses it to describe something that happens to a person, not something they work up in themselves.


It’s not a style of Christianity. It’s the entry point for all of it – as transformative as being born the first time.

elements.passageText: <sup>25</sup>I will sprinkle clean water on you and you will be clean from all your impurities. I will purify you from all your idols. <sup>26</sup>I will give you a new heart, and I will put a new spirit within you. I will remove the heart of stone from your body and give you a heart of flesh. <sup>27</sup>I will put my Spirit within you; I will take the initiative and you will obey my statutes and carefully observe my ordinances.

elements.question: What does God promise to do for his people?
How much of the initiative is God’s and how much is ours?

elements.text: Centuries before Jesus, God had already promised this: not gradual improvement, but transformation from the inside out. This is not self-reformation. It is divine heart surgery.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>The wind blows wherever it will, and you hear the sound it makes, but do not know where it comes from and where it is going. So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit.”

elements.question: How does Jesus describe the work of the Spirit?
What does this teach us about human control over new birth?

elements.text: Entry into the kingdom needs new birth. The wind is real - you can see the trees bend, feel it on your face – but you can’t see the wind itself, or control where it blows. The Spirit’s work of new birth is like that. Its effects are visible, but the work is God’s, not ours.

elements.passageText: “I tell you the solemn truth, the one who hears my word and believes the one who sent me has eternal life and will not be condemned, but has crossed over from death to life.

elements.question: What does Jesus promise to the one who hears and believes?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been fathered by God, and everyone who loves the father loves the child fathered by him.

elements.question: What does believing that Jesus is the Christ show about us?
How does faith being evidence of new birth guard us from pride (‘I was wise enough to believe’) and despair (‘My faith is weak’)?

elements.text: New birth is life from the dead. If you find yourself trusting in Christ – even weakly, even with doubts – that trust shows something has already happened in you. You didn’t generate it from nowhere. God gave it. Faith is the fruit of new birth, not the root of it.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>The hand of the LORD was on me, and he brought me out by the Spirit of the LORD and set me down in the middle of the valley; it was full of bones. <sup>2</sup>He made me walk all around among them. I realized there were a great many bones in the valley and they were very dry. <sup>3</sup>He said to me, “Son of man, can these bones live?” I said to him, “Sovereign LORD, you know.” <sup>4</sup>Then he said to me, “Prophesy over these bones, and tell them: ‘Dry bones, hear the word of the LORD. <sup>5</sup>This is what the Sovereign LORD says to these bones: Look, I am about to infuse breath into you and you will live. <sup>6</sup>I will put tendons on you and muscles over you and cover you with skin; I will put breath in you and you will live. Then you will know that I am the LORD.’” <sup>7</sup>So I prophesied as I was commanded. There was a sound when I prophesied — I heard a rattling, and the bones came together, bone to bone. <sup>8</sup>As I watched, I saw tendons on them, then muscles appeared, and skin covered over them from above, but there was no breath in them. <sup>9</sup>He said to me, “Prophesy to the breath, — prophesy, son of man — and tell the breath: ‘This is what the Sovereign LORD says: Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe on these corpses so that they will live.’” <sup>10</sup>So I prophesied as I was commanded, and the breath came into them; they lived and stood on their feet, an extremely large army.

elements.question: What question does God ask, and why is it significant?
What does this teach us about the source of spiritual life?

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For God, who said “Let light shine out of darkness,” is the one who shined in our hearts to give us the light of the glorious knowledge of God in the face of Christ.

elements.question: What comparison does Paul make with creation?
How is this ‘shining’ more than just learning new information?

elements.text: Regeneration is not just improvement – it is creation. The God who said ‘Let there be light’ must also shine into dark hearts.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>For the love of Christ controls us, since we have concluded this, that Christ died for all; therefore all have died. <sup>15</sup>And he died for all so that those who live should no longer live for themselves but for him who died for them and was raised.

elements.question: What change of direction does new birth bring? And why?
What does this mean for the ‘engine’ of Christian obedience?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>Dear friends, let us love one another, because love is from God, and everyone who loves has been fathered by God and knows God.

elements.question: What does love reveal about someone’s spiritual birth?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>The person who does not love does not know God, because God is love. <sup>9</sup>By this the love of God is revealed in us: that God has sent his one and only Son into the world so that we may live through him. <sup>10</sup>In this is love: not that we have loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the atoning sacrifice for our sins. <sup>11</sup>Dear friends, if God so loved us, we also ought to love one another. <sup>12</sup>No one has seen God at any time. If we love one another, God resides in us, and his love is perfected in us.

elements.question: Why is love evidence of new life?

elements.text: Regeneration changes what we love. The new birth produces a new love for God and for his people, a new sensitivity to sin, and a new hunger for righteousness. Not perfectly – but genuinely.

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>You have purified your souls by obeying the truth in order to show sincere mutual love. So love one another earnestly from a pure heart. <sup>23</sup>You have been born anew, not from perishable but from imperishable seed, through the living and enduring word of God.

elements.question: What are believers said to be born through?
So what is the relationship between the Spirit and the gospel?

elements.text:
God’s Spirit works through God’s word. Regeneration isn’t emotional intensity, a signed card, church attendance, or following the right rules. It’s an inward transformation that works its way out.

New birth is God’s promise and God’s work. We don’t produce it, any more than a newborn chooses to be born. But it shows up: a hunger for God that wasn’t there before; a sensitivity to sin so that you want to confess rather than hide; a love for other believers that surprises you. Not perfectly, and not all at once – but genuinely.

If you find those things stirring in you, even faintly, that is not something you generated. God is at work. And if you are thinking right now of someone whose heart seems completely closed to God – hold onto this: so did yours, once. No one is beyond the reach of the Spirit who brings the dead to life. Pray for them. Keep speaking. The wind blows where it will.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Does knowing that regeneration is God’s work give you more confidence or less? Are there ways in which you have treated your spiritual life as something you have to maintain by effort?
Have you ever had a period of outward religious activity (going to church, reading your Bible, behaving well) without much genuine inner life? What was that like? What was missing?
Where have you seen evidence of God’s transforming work in your own life – in your desires, relationships, or response to sin? Is there an area where you are still hoping for that change?

elements.text:
Marcus became a Christian in his twenties, largely through reading the Bible on his own. For the first few months, he kept it private. He prayed alone, read alone, and told no one. He thought of his faith as something between him and God.

Then a friend invited him to a small group. He resisted for months – but eventually went. He was surprised to find that he needed them, and they seemed to need him too. He hadn’t expected that.

Many people think of conversion as purely personal – a ‘personal relationship’ between them and God – and this is genuinely true. But in the New Testament, those who come to Christ are not left spiritually alone. When God gives someone a new heart, he also places them in a new family – among brothers and sisters who belong to the same Father.

elements.passageText: <sup>41</sup>So those who accepted his message were baptized, and that day about three thousand people were added. <sup>42</sup>They were devoting themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer.

elements.question: What marked the shared life of those who received the word?
What does this suggest is the normal pattern after conversion?

elements.text: In the New Testament, conversion is never merely private. It is always visible. It produces belonging, shared worship, shared life.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>For just as the body is one and yet has many members, and all the members of the body — though many — are one body, so too is Christ. <sup>13</sup>For in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body. Whether Jews or Greeks or slaves or free, we were all made to drink of the one Spirit.

elements.question: What image describes the church? How do people enter it?
What does this passage teach about unity among believers?

elements.text: The church is not a voluntary association of like-minded individuals. It is a body formed by Christ. To be joined to Christ is to be joined to his body. There is no category in the New Testament for a Christian without a church.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>So as you come to him, a living stone rejected by men but chosen and precious in God’s sight, <sup>5</sup>you yourselves, as living stones, are built up as a spiritual house to be a holy priesthood and to offer spiritual sacrifices that are acceptable to God through Jesus Christ.

elements.question: How are believers described? What are they being built into?
What purpose does this structure serve?

elements.text: Conversion means being made part of what God is building. Christians are not isolated stones, scattered about. They are being built together – into God’s house.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Do not become partners with those who do not believe, for what partnership is there between righteousness and lawlessness, or what fellowship does light have with darkness? <sup>15</sup>And what agreement does Christ have with Beliar? Or what does a believer share in common with an unbeliever? <sup>16</sup>And what mutual agreement does the temple of God have with idols? For we are the temple of the living God, just as God said, “I will live in them and will walk among them, and I will be their God, and they will be my people.” <sup>17</sup>Therefore “come out from their midst, and be separate,” says the Lord, “and touch no unclean thing, and I will welcome you, <sup>18</sup>and I will be a father to you, and you will be my sons and daughters,” says the All-Powerful Lord. 7:1 Therefore, since we have these promises, dear friends, let us cleanse ourselves from everything that could defile the flesh and the spirit, and thus accomplish holiness out of reverence for God.

elements.question: What identity does God claim for his people?
What call flows from that identity?

elements.text: The church is not just gathered; it is set apart. Conversion brings us into a holy community – one defined not by culture, ethnicity, or shared taste, but by belonging to God. That is a stranger and more demanding bond than any of those.

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works, <sup>25</sup>not abandoning our own meetings, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day drawing near.

elements.question: What are believers commanded to consider?
What dangers are implied if we neglect to meet together?

elements.text: The church’s life together includes mutual responsibility. Love in the church is not sentimental. It is committed, corrective, and persevering.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>“If your brother sins, go and show him his fault when the two of you are alone. If he listens to you, you have regained your brother. <sup>16</sup>But if he does not listen, take one or two others with you, so that at the testimony of two or three witnesses every matter may be established. <sup>17</sup>If he refuses to listen to them, tell it to the church. If he refuses to listen to the church, treat him like a Gentile or a tax collector.

elements.question: What steps are outlined when a brother sins?
What does this passage assume about visible belonging?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>So I exhort the elders among you as a fellow elder and a witness of Christ’s sufferings and as one who shares in the glory that will be revealed: <sup>2</sup>Give a shepherd’s care to God’s flock among you, exercising oversight not merely as a duty but willingly under God’s direction, not for shameful profit but eagerly. <sup>3</sup>And do not lord it over those entrusted to you, but be examples to the flock.

elements.question: How are elders to shepherd? What motivations are ruled out?

elements.text:
Christ gives leaders to shepherd his people. Church membership is not merely organisational. It is relational and pastoral. Leaders care for souls. Members are not customers. Together, imperfectly, they are meant to look like Christ.

God does not save us and then leave us alone. He places us in a family – with all the mess, the closeness, the obligation, and the joy that families involve. The church is not a club you join when it suits you, or a service you attend when you feel like it. It is your family. You belong to these people, and they belong to you.

That may feel uncomfortable if church has been disappointing, or if belonging has felt costly. It often is costly. But the alternative – a private Christianity, lived in isolation from the body – is not on offer in the New Testament. You were not saved to be a scattered stone. You were saved to be built into something, together with others, for God’s purposes in the world.

So: where do you belong? Not just where do you attend – but where are you truly known, and truly committed? If you don’t have an answer to that yet, this chapter is an invitation to find one. Conversion is personal, but it is never private: God saves us to be a people.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Have you ever treated church involvement as optional: attending when convenient, keeping yourself at a certain distance? What were your reasons? How do you feel about those reasons now?
In your own experience, are you more of an attender or a belonger? What would it look like – practically, this week – to move further toward genuine belonging?

Is there a person in your church community you have been avoiding, or a level of commitment you have been resisting? What would it mean to take seriously that this person – and this community – is your family?

elements.text:
Imagine two trees standing side by side. From a distance they look the same: same height, same leaves, same shape. But when harvest comes, one tree is filled with fruit and the other has none. What looked similar on the outside had different things going on inside.

Sofia had been in church her whole life. She knew the right words, attended every Sunday, and was well liked. But privately, nothing had changed. Her temper still flared at home; she still harboured bitterness she’d never confessed; she still lived as if God’s opinion of her choices didn’t really matter. She wasn’t sure whether she had ever truly come to Christ, or whether she had just become good at looking like she had.

Genuine faith eventually produces visible fruit. That doesn’t mean Christians are perfect – but conversion doesn’t leave a life unchanged.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The word ‘sanctification’ rarely appears in conversation, but the idea is straightforward: becoming more holy over time. The misunderstanding is treating it as a self-improvement project – trying harder, behaving better, managing sin.


But sanctification is more like a plant growing than a machine being upgraded. The life is already there. The task is staying connected to the source, not generating growth through willpower.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>But I say, live by the Spirit and you will not carry out the desires of the flesh. <sup>17</sup>For the flesh has desires that are opposed to the Spirit, and the Spirit has desires that are opposed to the flesh, for these are in opposition to each other, so that you cannot do what you want.

elements.question: What conflict does Paul describe in these verses?
Why do believers still experience this struggle?

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, <sup>23</sup>gentleness, and self-control. Against such things there is no law.

elements.question: What qualities are described as the fruit of the Spirit?
Which of these qualities most challenge or encourage you now?

elements.text: The fruit of the Spirit is not achieved. It is grown. The Spirit is already at work in you – reshaping what you want, what you reach for, what you love.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Now this is the gospel message we have heard from him and announce to you: God is light, and in him there is no darkness at all. <sup>6</sup>If we say we have fellowship with him and yet keep on walking in the darkness, we are lying and not practicing the truth. <sup>7</sup>But if we walk in the light as he himself is in the light, we have fellowship with one another and the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin.

elements.question: What does John say about God’s nature?
What does it mean to ‘walk in the light’?
Why is honesty about sin an important mark of genuine faith?

elements.text: A converted person doesn’t pretend to be sinless. But they increasingly refuse to live in darkness.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Do not lie to one another since you have put off the old man with its practices <sup>10</sup>and have been clothed with the new man that is being renewed in knowledge according to the image of the one who created it.

elements.question: What have believers ‘put off’ and ‘put on’?
How is the new self being renewed?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>So then, my dear friends, just as you have always obeyed, not only in my presence but even more in my absence, continue working out your salvation with awe and reverence, <sup>13</sup>for the one bringing forth in you both the desire and the effort — for the sake of his good pleasure — is God.

elements.question: What are believers commanded to do?
How does this fit with the reason Paul gives in v13?

elements.text: God works in us – and we work. Both are true, and neither cancels out the other. The fact that God is at work in you is not a reason to be passive. It is the reason you can dare to try.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>The person who has my commandments and obeys them is the one who loves me. The one who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love him and will reveal myself to him."

elements.question: What is the relationship between love and obedience?
Why does love change the nature of obedience?

elements.text: There is a right way to fear God. But that is not what drives Christian obedience at its heart. Christian obedience is driven by love for Christ. That is a different engine entirely.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its desires, <sup>13</sup>and do not present your members to sin as instruments to be used for unrighteousness, but present yourselves to God as those who are alive from the dead and your members to God as instruments to be used for righteousness. <sup>14</sup>For sin will have no mastery over you, because you are not under law but under grace.

elements.question: What command does Paul give regarding sin? And why?
What does it mean to be ‘not under law but under grace’?

elements.text: Conversion does not eliminate temptation; it breaks sin’s rule.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>See to it, brothers and sisters, that none of you has an evil, unbelieving heart that forsakes the living God. <sup>13</sup>But exhort one another each day, as long as it is called "Today," that none of you may become hardened by sin’s deception. <sup>14</sup>For we have become partners with Christ, if in fact we hold our initial confidence firm until the end.

elements.question: What warning is given to believers?
What role do fellow believers play in perseverance?

elements.text: Endurance doesn’t cause salvation, but it is evidence of it.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>But the path of the righteous is like the bright morning light, growing brighter and brighter until full day.

elements.question: What image is used to describe the path of the righteous? How does this image capture the process of spiritual growth?

elements.text:
Growth in holiness is often gradual, like a sunrise that you don’t notice moment by moment, but which has filled the sky by the time you look up. You may not feel like you are changing. But look back over five years, and ask honestly: are your desires different? Is sin more uncomfortable than it used to be? Do you love what you once ignored?

The same grace that brought you to Christ is at work in you now, reshaping you from the inside. You are not meant to do this by willpower alone – the Spirit is in you for exactly this purpose. Where growth feels painfully slow, don’t give up and don’t pretend. Bring it honestly to God. He who began a good work in you has not abandoned it. Christians are not perfect – but because of what Christ has done, and is doing, they are genuinely, lastingly new.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Is there a way in which you have been trying to produce fruit – better behaviour, more kindness – without attending to the roots? What would attending to the roots look like for you now?
Which aspect of the fruit of the Spirit do you most long to see grow in your life, and which do people who know you well most noticeably see lacking? Are those the same thing, or different?
Is there an area of your life where growth has felt slow or stalled? Have you been tempted to give up, or to stop thinking about it? What would it mean to bring that area before God?

elements.text:
David had been a Christian – or thought he had – for fifteen years. He had been baptised, led a small group, and even preached occasionally. Then, in a period of serious illness, he started to ask himself honest questions. He had all the right answers. But when he looked at his life, he couldn’t find much that had actually changed. He had kept the outward form of Christianity without much of the inner reality.

The Bible speaks honestly to anyone who finds themselves asking David’s question – whether the answer turns out to be reassuring or unsettling. It offers both comfort and warning, sometimes in the same passage.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Some assume that doubting your salvation is spiritual humility – that being too sure means you’re taking grace for granted. Others assume that saying ‘I’m saved’ is simply what Christians say, regardless of what their life shows.


Neither is quite right. Biblical assurance isn’t presumption or performance – it’s a confidence grounded in what Christ has done, witnessed by the Spirit, and evidenced by genuine change.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>And every priest stands day after day serving and offering the same sacrifices again and again — sacrifices that can never remove sins. <sup>12</sup>But when this priest had offered one sacrifice for sins for all time, he sat down at the right hand of God, <sup>13</sup>where he is now waiting until his enemies are made a footstool for his feet. <sup>14</sup>For by one offering he has perfected for all time those who are made holy.

elements.question: What did Jesus achieve, and what confidence does this give us?

elements.text: Christians are not meant to live in constant uncertainty about their relationship with God. Our confidence begins – and ends – with what Christ has done. Not with how we feel.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>For you did not receive the spirit of slavery leading again to fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry, “Abba, Father.” <sup>16</sup>The Spirit himself bears witness to our spirit that we are God’s children.

elements.question: What testimony does the Spirit give?

elements.passageText: 3Now by this we know that we have come to know God: if we keep his commandments. 4The one who says “I have come to know God” and yet does not keep his commandments is a liar, and the truth is not in such a person. 5But whoever obeys his word, truly the love of God has been perfected in him. By this we know that we are in him: the one who says he resides in God ought himself to walk just as Jesus walked.

elements.question: According to John, how do we know that we know God?

elements.text: The Spirit also testifies that we belong to God. And assurance grows as we see God’s work in our lives. Changed lives do not earn salvation. But they are evidence that salvation is real.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! By his great mercy he gave us new birth into a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, <sup>4</sup>that is, into an inheritance imperishable, undefiled, and unfading. It is reserved in heaven for you, <sup>5</sup>who are being protected by God’s power through faith for a salvation ready to be revealed in the last time.

elements.question: What hope, and what inheritance, has God given believers?
How are believers being guarded until the final salvation?

elements.text: True believers continue. Not because they are strong, but because God keeps them. He does not only save his people. He holds them.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>And you were at one time strangers and enemies in your minds as expressed through your evil deeds, <sup>22</sup>but now he has reconciled you by his physical body through death to present you holy, without blemish, and blameless before him — <sup>23</sup>if indeed you remain in the faith, established and firm, without shifting from the hope of the gospel that you heard.

elements.question: What change has taken place for believers?
What does it mean to remain ‘stable and steadfast’?

elements.text: Believers are repeatedly urged to continue. Perseverance does not earn salvation. It shows that salvation is real.

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>And let us hold unwaveringly to the hope we confess, for the one who made the promise is trustworthy. <sup>24</sup>And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works, <sup>25</sup>not abandoning our own meetings, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day drawing near.

elements.question: What role does meeting together play in perseverance?

elements.text: God uses the Christian community to sustain believers. Christians are not meant to persevere alone.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>“Now the parable means this: The seed is the word of God. <sup>12</sup>Those along the path are the ones who have heard; then the devil comes and takes away the word from their hearts, so that they may not believe and be saved. <sup>13</sup>Those on the rock are the ones who receive the word with joy when they hear it, but they have no root. They believe for a while, but in a time of testing fall away. <sup>14</sup>As for the seed that fell among thorns, these are the ones who hear, but as they go on their way they are choked by the worries and riches and pleasures of life, and their fruit does not ripen. <sup>15</sup>But as for the seed that landed on good soil, these are the ones who, after hearing the word, cling to it with an honest and good heart, and bear fruit with steadfast endurance.

elements.question: Why does seed on the rocky ground fail, but grow in good soil?

elements.text: Scripture is honest: not every response to the gospel is genuine. Some fall away. The soil matters.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>They profess to know God but with their deeds they deny him, since they are detestable, disobedient, and unfit for any good deed.

elements.question: Why is this warning important for the church?

elements.text: Words alone do not prove genuine faith. Anyone can say the right things. What cannot be faked, over time, is a transformed life.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>“Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter into the kingdom of heaven — only the one who does the will of my Father in heaven. <sup>22</sup>On that day, many will say to me, ‘Lord, Lord, didn’t we prophesy in your name, and in your name cast out demons and do many powerful deeds?’ <sup>23</sup>Then I will declare to them, ‘I never knew you. Go away from me, you lawbreakers!’

elements.question: What does Matthew say about the danger of false assurance?

elements.text: You can be outwardly religious, and yet Christ doesn’t know you. That is a terrifying possibility. Religious activity isn’t the same as knowing Christ.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Put yourselves to the test to see if you are in the faith; examine yourselves! Or do you not recognize regarding yourselves that Jesus Christ is in you — unless, indeed, you fail the test!

elements.question: What does Paul tell believers to do?

elements.text:
This chapter holds two things together; don’t let go of either. If you are trusting in Christ – even weakly, even with doubts – you have real grounds for assurance. Not because your faith is strong, but because the One you are trusting is. His death is sufficient. His Spirit witnesses to your adoption. The changed desires you notice in yourself, however imperfect, are evidence of his work. You do not have to live in constant uncertainty. You are held.

But the Bible is also honest: not every profession of faith is real. Religious activity, church attendance, and correct answers are not the same as knowing Christ. If something in this chapter has unsettled you – if the warning passages landed more heavily than the assurance passages – don’t brush that aside. Bring it to God. Ask him to show you whether what you have is real, and to make it real if it’s not. That prayer will not go unanswered. He does not turn away anyone who comes to him in genuine need.

The goal of self-examination is not despair. It is to drive you – or bring you back – to Christ himself. That is always the right destination.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: When you think about your own standing before God, do you struggle more with false assurance (‘I’m fine’) or with doubt (‘I’m probably not saved’)? What feeds that tendency in you?
2 Corinthians 13:5 says, ‘Examine yourselves to see whether you are in the faith.’ As you do that honestly: what do you find? What gives you confidence – and what gives you pause?

Is there anything in this chapter (a warning, a passage, a description of false faith) that has unsettled you? Bring it honestly to God: is this the Spirit convicting you toward genuine faith, or the enemy trying to undermine real assurance? How can you tell the difference?

elements.text:
Suppose you were a doctor, and you were sent to an epidemic with a cure for a deadly disease. Your task is not to design clever advertising campaigns or repackage the medicine to make it fashionable. People’s lives depend on something far simpler: that the cure be clearly explained and offered.

The gospel is like that. It is not a product that needs clever marketing. It is the announcement of what God has done in Christ to save sinners. When Christians share the gospel, our task is not to sell something attractive, but to faithfully tell the truth about the cure God has provided.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The word ‘evangelism’ tends to conjure images of confrontational strangers, high-pressure tactics, or slick presentations. But the word simply means announcing good news.


The task isn’t to sell, persuade, or close a deal – it’s to tell the truth clearly about what God has done in Christ, summon people to respond, and trust God to do his work. Less salesperson. More witness.

elements.passageText: <sup>42</sup>And every day both in the temple courts and from house to house, they did not stop teaching and proclaiming the good news that Jesus is the Christ.

elements.question: What did the apostles continually do, and where did they do it?
Who was the focus of their message?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When I came to you, brothers and sisters, I did not come with superior eloquence or wisdom as I proclaimed the testimony of God. <sup>2</sup>For I decided to be concerned about nothing among you except Jesus Christ, and him crucified.

elements.question: What did Paul emphasise when preaching in Corinth?
What danger does this passage warn against in evangelism?

elements.text: At the centre of evangelism is a person: Jesus Christ. Not our story. Not our moral advice. Not our religious experience. Christ – who he is, and what he has done.

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>Was it not necessary for the Christ to suffer these things and enter into his glory?” <sup>27</sup>Then beginning with Moses and all the prophets, he interpreted to them the things written about himself in all the scriptures.

elements.question: What does Jesus explain to the disciples?
What is the role of Scripture in understanding Christ?

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>Paul went to the Jews in the synagogue, as he customarily did, and on three Sabbath days he addressed them from the scriptures, <sup>3</sup>explaining and demonstrating that the Christ had to suffer and to rise from the dead, saying, “This Jesus I am proclaiming to you is the Christ.”

elements.question: What method does Paul use? What does he conclude about Jesus?

elements.text: The gospel is rooted in God’s word. We didn’t invent it. We open Scripture, and show where it points: always, from every angle, to Christ.

elements.passageText: 19Therefore repent and turn back so that your sins may be wiped out, so that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord.

elements.question: What command, and what promise, does Peter give?
What does this tell us about the urgency of the gospel?

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>He said, “The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe the gospel!”

elements.question: What announcement is made? What responses are called for?

elements.text: Evangelism does not stop at explanation. It calls for a response. The gospel is not information to consider. It is a summons to obey.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>Therefore, because we know the fear of the Lord, we try to persuade people. We are well known to God, and I hope we are well known to your consciences too.

elements.question: Why is evangelism serious? What should motivate us?

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>Instead we have renounced shameful hidden deeds, not behaving with deceptiveness or distorting the word of God, but by open proclamation of the truth we commend ourselves to everyone’s conscience before God.

elements.question: What methods does Paul reject?

elements.text: The apostles urged people to believe — but without manipulation, without pressure, without tricks. Persuasion is legitimate. Pressure is not. The difference matters.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>And when he comes, he will prove the world wrong concerning sin and righteousness and judgment — <sup>9</sup>concerning sin, because they do not believe in me; <sup>10</sup>concerning righteousness, because I am going to the Father and you will see me no longer; <sup>11</sup>and concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world has been condemned.

elements.question: What work does the Spirit perform in the world?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>We know, brothers and sisters loved by God, that he has chosen you, <sup>5</sup>in that our gospel did not come to you merely in words, but in power and in the Holy Spirit and with deep conviction (surely you recall the character we displayed when we were among you for your benefit).

elements.question: What was the Spirit’s role in the gospel coming to Thessalonica?
Because conversion is God’s work, what does this mean for our evangelism?

elements.text: Because conversion is God’s work, evangelism depends on the Spirit’s power. The Spirit opens hearts, brings conviction, gives new life. We cannot do any of that. What we can do is speak. So speak.

elements.passageText: 24And the Lord’s slave must not engage in heated disputes but be kind toward all, an apt teacher, patient, 25correcting opponents with gentleness. Perhaps God will grant them repentance and then knowledge of the truth and they will come to their senses and escape the devil’s trap where they are held captive to do his will.

elements.question: What kind of attitude should the Lord’s servant have?
Why does evangelism require patience?

elements.text:
Evangelism requires patience. God works in his own time, not ours. Our job is faithfulness, gentleness, and perseverance. The harvest is his.

The gospel isn’t a product that needs clever packaging. You are not a salesperson. You are someone who has received a cure for a fatal disease – and you know people who are dying. That changes things. It means your job is not to be impressive, or persuasive, or to close a deal. Your job is to be honest and faithful: to tell people clearly what God has done in Christ, to call them to repent and believe, and then to leave the results with the God who opens hearts.

The pressure is off you, because the work was never yours to do alone. As you finish this course, there is one question worth pondering: Who is the person God has put in front of you – a friend, a family member, a colleague – who needs to hear this? You don’t need a special gift or a perfect moment. You need willingness. The Spirit will do the rest.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Have you ever felt pressure – from yourself, from a church culture, or from a specific situation – to push someone toward a decision rather than simply tell them the truth? What happened? What would you do differently?
When you last had a conversation about faith with someone who isn’t a Christian, what did you talk about? Was Christ and what he has done central – or did you drift to something easier?

Be honest: does the thought of talking to someone about Jesus make you anxious? What specifically worries you most – rejection, getting it wrong, damaging a relationship? How does what you’ve learned in this chapter speak to that specific fear?

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.
